short summary: Papyrus Bodmer XX is an early fourth-century Greek manuscript containing the 'Apology of Phileas,' which details the trial and martyrdom of the Bishop of Thmuis. During his interrogation, Phileas explicitly affirms that Christ is God despite the Roman prefect's challenges regarding His crucifixion. This text serves as early documentary evidence of high Christology and the worship of Jesus as divine prior to the Council of Nicaea.
Description: Papyrus Bodmer XX, discovered in Egypt as part of the Bodmer Papyri, is a Greek manuscript from the early fourth century containing the 'Apology of Phileas'. It records the trial of Phileas, Bishop of Thmuis, during the Diocletianic persecutions (c. 304-306 AD) under the Roman prefect Clodius Culcianus. The text is crucial for understanding early Christology as it features a direct dialogue where the prefect asks Phileas if Christ is God, and Phileas unhesitatingly affirms it. Culcianus questions how a God could be crucified, to which Phileas responds that Jesus willingly submitted to the cross for human salvation, maintaining His divine nature. This manuscript provides clear evidence that early Christians worshipped Jesus as God well before the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, showing that the deity of Christ was a deeply entrenched belief and not a later political invention.

quotes.quote:
Κουλκιανός: Θεὸς ἦν ὁ Χριστός;
Φιλέας: Ναί.
quotes.translation:
Culcianus: Was Christ God?
Phileas: Yes.

quotes.quote:
Κουλκιανός: Πῶς οὖν σταυροῦται θεὸς ὤν;
Φιλέας: Ὑπὲρ τῆς ἡμῶν σωτηρίας... (ἐσταυρώθη)
quotes.translation:
Culcianus: How could a God be crucified?
Phileas: For our salvation He willingly suffered and was crucified.

Title: PGM XIII Command
short summary: The PGM XIII Command is a 4th-century CE magical papyrus from Egypt containing spells that invoke the name of Jesus alongside supreme deities. The inclusion of Jesus's name by non-Christian syncretic magicians demonstrates that His reputation as a powerful divine figure was widely recognized outside orthodox Christian circles. This provides independent historical evidence that early Christians worshipped Jesus as a divine being with supreme cosmic authority.
Description: The PGM XIII Command, part of the Greek Magical Papyri (Papyri Graecae Magicae, or PGM), is a text dating to the mid-fourth century CE, discovered in Thebes, Egypt, as part of the "Theban Magical Library." The document compiles syncretic magical spells, incantations, and formulas that blend Egyptian, Greco-Roman, Jewish, and Christian elements. A notable feature of this papyrus is the invocation of "Jesus" as a word of power, alongside supreme deities like Yahweh (Iao), Thoth, and Zeus, to command spirits and cast out demons. Ancient syncretic magicians sought the most powerful names to compel the unseen realm, indicating that Jesus's name was viewed as carrying supreme, cosmic authority. This use of Jesus's name by pagan or non-orthodox practitioners provides external, independent historical evidence of high Christology. It demonstrates that the belief in the divinity and supreme authority of Jesus was so widely established in the ancient Mediterranean world that even those outside the Christian faith attempted to harness His perceived divine power. It was originally discovered or kept at: Thebes (modern-day Luxor, Egypt).

quotes.quote: ἄκουσόν μου, Χριστέ
quotes.translation: Hear me, O Christ

Title: The Michigan Magical Papyrus
short summary: The Michigan Magical Papyrus (P. Mich. 136) is a 4th-century CE syncretic amulet from Egypt that invokes Jesus as a divine figure alongside the God of Israel. It demonstrates that even outside formal church structures, Jesus was recognized and invoked as a powerful deity, showing he was functionally worshipped as God.
Description: The Michigan Magical Papyrus (specifically P. Mich. 136) dates to the 4th century CE and was discovered in the Fayum region of Egypt. It belongs to a broader collection of magical papyri that reflect the highly syncretic religious environment of Graeco-Roman Egypt, where practitioners blended Egyptian, Greek, Jewish, and Christian elements to create protective amulets and spells. In these texts, the name of Jesus is utilized as a "word of power" ("nomen sacrum") and is invoked directly to heal, protect, and command spirits. The magicians invoked Him alongside the God of Israel (Iao/Yahweh), sometimes referring to Him as "the God Jesus." This "ground-level" evidence shows that the belief in the divinity of Jesus was not merely a dogma established by late church councils, but a widespread popular belief. The fact that even syncretic practitioners recognized His supreme cosmic authority and invoked Him as a deity to cast out demons demonstrates that Christ was practically and functionally worshipped as divine from a very early period. It was originally discovered or kept at: Fayum region of Egypt.

quotes.quote: ⲡⲛⲟⲩⲧⲉ ⲓⲏⲥⲟⲩⲥ / ὁ θεὸς Ἰησοῦς
quotes.translation: the God Jesus

Title: Early Christian Bronze Lamps
short summary: Early Christian bronze lamps from Late Antiquity served both practical and theological purposes, incorporating explicit Christian symbols like the Chi-Rho and Ichthys (fish). These symbols, integrated into household items that provided light, acted as daily confessions of faith in Jesus Christ as the divine 'Light of the World' and the 'Son of God, Savior.' The widespread domestic use of these lamps demonstrates that the belief in Jesus's divinity was deeply integrated into the daily lives of ordinary Christians.
Description:
During the early centuries of the Church, particularly after the legalization of Christianity in the fourth century, the material culture of everyday Christians began to reflect their theological convictions. Early Christian bronze lamps, dating primarily from the 3rd to 6th centuries AD and found across the Late Roman and Byzantine empires, are prime examples of this phenomenon. Excavated from both homes and churches, these lamps served a dual purpose: providing physical light and expressing spiritual devotion.

Many of these lamps featured the Chi-Rho monogram (☧), representing the first two Greek letters of 'Christ' (ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ), often integrated into handles or covers. Placing this symbol on a light source made a clear theological statement: Christ was the divine light illuminating the world. Additionally, lamps were cast in zoomorphic shapes, such as the dove (representing the Holy Spirit) or the fish. The fish is especially significant due to the Greek word *Ichthys* (ΙΧΘΥΣ) serving as an acrostic for 'Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior.' By lighting these lamps, early Christians made a daily, visual affirmation of Jesus's divine sonship and Savior status, showing that the worship of Jesus as divine was a practical reality for ordinary believers, not just a topic for theological debates. It was originally discovered or kept at: Various regions across the Late Roman and Byzantine empires.

quotes.quote: ΙΧΘΥΣ
quotes.translation: Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior
